title: Blackout Protocol (LD57)
desc:
# Quick Notes

Blackout Protocol was the result of my first gamejam.
Ludum Dare 57.

The game was developed in under 72 hours (rougly 48 hours), and was built around the theme "Depths".

The game relied heavily on its atmosphere, specifically its graphics, sound design, and monster design. Gameplay was lacking, but you can't ask for too much from 48 hours.

I co-developed the game with Cooper Horne, and I covered all of the modeling, texturing, and the monsters many systems.
(eg: AI, pathfinding, patrolling)

# Monster Design

Most game jam monsters are thrown together as fast as possible. NavMesh + a state machine. Not this one.

I had never designed a proper, and well rounded monster before, so I wanted to make something special. Due to time constraints, research was short, and mostly fruitless. So I had to come up with my own method.

At a basic level, a good monster is a set of data, and rules, put together in a clever way to create good results.

To outline the monster from our game briefly:
# Data:
Distance to floor. (A)
Direct raycast to player. (clamped to an FOV to prevent any unfairness, and to let the player sneak past it.) (B)
Another array of raycasts in a line of 6 raycasts (at an FOV of around 270.) (C)
# Rules:
Maintain a constant height.
Rotate towards the direction you're moving.
Calculate a left/right ratio of what raycasts are touching cave walls, and adjust your direction accordingly.
If you see nothing, patrol between preset points (requires no pathfinding logic)

With this set of Data and Rules, out comes the monster from Blackout Protocol. It can pursue the player anywhere they run, even while being inside tight cave spaces. (hence why I was hesitant to try and use NavMesh)

Overall, it was a good bit of fun figuring him out.

# On Blackout Protocol

Blackout Protocol was a great learning experience, from being my first game jam (teaching me to work with a team, under tight time constraints, and still to ship something I can be proud of), to just improving my understanding of the Unity ECS, and emergent behaviour from data and rules.

The game is available on Itch below, and as of now there are no planned updates.

title: ONO/SOUL
desc:
# Quick Notes

ONO and its unreleased successor ONOSOUL were developed over around 6 months. ONO was made for the Alberta Game Jam 2025, and won by community vote.

ONOSOUL was meant to expand on the story and atmosphere the team felt was missing from the original: more world building, more feeling. It was never finished due to scope creep and burnout.

ONO is still available to play. I hope to return to ONOSOUL someday.

My favourite part has to be the soundtrack. Our sound guy really sold the feeling we were going for.

# SNAP (Scriptable Node Action Pipeline)

Puzzle logic in game jams gets hardcoded fast. One-off scripts, spaghetti conditions, nothing reusable. I built SNAP to fix that.

SNAP is a node graph runtime for Unity. Each node is a self-contained behaviour. Flow ports for execution, data ports for values. Graphs are Unity ScriptableAssets, so puzzle logic lives entirely outside code. Was a word formed? Was electricity turned on? Each condition is just a node. Wire them together, done. Nodes can be single conditions, composite logic, whatever you can dream up.

What made it different from standard behaviour trees is that graphs can be circular. Most systems enforce acyclic graphs by design. SNAP doesn't: if your puzzle needs a loop, you wire a loop.

Designers (just me lol) built graphs in an HTML editor rather than Unity's inspector since I hate writing C# GUI's, especially with Unity (at least it isn't Avalonia lol). It serialized directly to the ScriptableObject format. No code required to build a new puzzle.

All in all it was a good learning experience, and I found it had a lot of similarities to Nintendo's pracitices with their latest Zelda title: Tears of the Kingdom, and it's internal AINB (Artificial Intelligence Node Binary) system the game operates on.

# Sort Mode Hell

It was our first 2D game and we fought sorting the whole way through. Our first approach was vertex-based Y displacement with a 0.001 multiplier, plus a per-shader offset value to fine-tune individual objects. It worked, but it killed batching, and flickered on non-static objects.

We went through three or four methods after that. Eventually we found the solution was already built into Unity. We'd been solving a problem that didn't need solving. That one hurt a little, but it's the kind of thing you only learn by doing it wrong first.

# Notes from the Musician (Quinn DeBoer)

What started off as just a simple game jam became one of my favourite projects that I have ever worked on. ONOSOUL was about a lot of things, but what I loved the most was that it was about growth. As a sound designer and music producer, I learned a lot trying to create a calming, yet slightly eerie, lofi atmosphere. I grew a ton throughout the process, the same way that Ono had on their journey. I find myself reminiscing on these days occasionally. Although ONOSOUL was just sounds, pixel art, and code, something about its tenderness made me feel alive. It’s kind of ironic in hindsight that working with dead ghosts was what made that feeling bloom.


# On ONOSOUL

We planned many areas for the game, and one of my favourite ones is this area that completely lacked colour. It was meant to represent the lack of emotion the world of ONOSOUL had. ONOSOUL was a game about loss, but deeper than that: stagnation. As if the world had stopped moving forward.

ONOSOUL's ultimate lesson was and still is that:

it's OK to say goodbye.

Directly quoting the game document, it would have ended with: 'No big music swell. Just credits with a subtle track.'

media.caption: large graph in the HTML SNAP editor
small graph in the HTML SNAP editor
I think it might have been a sign.
quite the map I know (early screenshot)

title: Quantized Render Pipeline Extension
desc:
# Quick Notes

Our QRPE (Quantized Render Pipeline Extension) is a URP Renderer Feature that gives any Unity URP 17+ project a quantized (or optionally pixel art) aesthetic. Palette-quantized colours, a crisp pixel grid, and hybrid geometry-normal-aware outlines. All under one checkbox in the renderer asset, with zero changes to any scene materials.
(it also integrates directly with our Volumetric Fog system which is covered in its own article)

# Method To The Madness

Although it may sound a bit complicated, the methods we have chosen to use are quite simple.

The pipeline runs four passes in sequence:
Downsample -> Quantize -> Outline -> Upscale

The scene is first point-sampled down to a target res (low or full depending on the look you want for your game, default 320x180).

Point filtering is very important: any interpolation would blur the pixel grid before we ever reach the quantization pass, and then everything would fall apart and become messy. All of the other steps happen at this target resolution, and in the case that you're using a low res setting, it keeps costs to a minimum.

Then, to finish off, everything gets point sampled back up to the screen's resolution, which gives every texel a clean uniform block (or corresponding) pixel. No blending, or smoothing: by design.

Quantization and outlines both run at the target res on purpose: Outlines drawn at 320x180 and then upscaled look proper, outlines drawn at 1080p and then downscaled are an antialiasing mess.






# CIE Lab Colour Matching

This is the part that most quantization systems miss, but it's well worth talking about.

Short-sighted palette quantization finds the nearest colour by Euclidean distance in RGB space. However, RGB distance is a terrible proxy for visual similarity: it treats red to blue, and dark to light as equivalent distances, which they clearly aren't to a human eye. The result is incorrect palette snapping on saturated colours and muddy gradients.











QRPE converts every pixel to CIE Lab space before searching the palette. Lab is a perceptual colour space: distance in Lab correlates directly with how different two colours look to a human. The L channel is lightness, a is green to red, b is blue to yellow. A distance of 1 Lab unit is roughly the smallest difference a trained observer can detect. The palette search finds the nearest entry by Lab distance, then converts back to RGB for output. Colours snap to visually correct palette entries rather than mathematically convenient ones.











Ordered Bayer dithering runs before the palette snap, nudging each pixel's Lab value by a small offset from a 4x4 matrix. Because the dither is applied in Lab space, it adds subtle surface variation that looks like texture instead of a colour error.

# Outlines

The outline pass runs a 4-neighbor depth comparison at each pixel. Where the depth delta between a pixel and its neighboring ones exceeds a threshold in the config, an outline is drawn. Depth-only outlines miss surface details on flat geometry (eg: a sphere reads as a single silhouette with no interior edges), so normal-based detection runs alongside the main outline system. The center pixel's normal is compared against the average of its neighbors; where they diverge significantly: is considered a surface difference worth outlining.


# The Palette System

I don't have too much to say regarding this, it's simple for a reason.
Palettes are just a Color[] array on a ScriptableObject, which means you can hotswap the game's colour mood at runtime by swapping the asset (with zero shader recompilation). The default palette has 64 colours across 4 brightness levels, and 16 hues.

We also developed a "palette designer" tool in Unity, that generates palettes from parameters like hue count, brightness range, saturation range, and per-level hue shifts.



# Final Notes

Honestly, this is one of my top 5 shader effects I've ever developed, I had tons of fun learning the science behind it, and the inspiration for it was from a YouTube video I got recommended, so I'll leave it as a link below this article in case you're interested.

media.caption: a fishing capybara from the project that first used the pipeline.
no idea why this example is so misaligned lol
example palette I found online to fill the empty space lol.

title: FFT Ocean System
desc:
# Quick Notes

This covers the FFT ocean system I developed for our studio whilst participating in the Game Name Game Jam 2026.
Although we did not end up submitting our project, the FFT ocean I decided to do on a whim, is definitely worth showing off, because the math behind it, as well as the result are both really interesting.
(scope creep sucks lol)

# The Math

In our implementation (and any FFT ocean), it's not simulated wave by wave. Instead, the surface is described statistically as a distribution of energy, across frequencies and directions. This is called the JONSWAP spectrum, which is a model developed for real oceanographic use, originally for naval engineering (who would've thought it would be used to simulate the ocean in an unreleased indie title lol).
It takes inputs like wind speed, fetch (how far the wind has travelled over the water), and a peak enhancement factor, and outputs how much energy each wave frequency should carry.

This spectrum lives in the frequency domain. To actually get a surface out of it, you run a Inverse Fast Fourier Transform every frame on the GPU, which converts the frequency-domain description into real vertex displacements across a 512x512 grid (Render Texture) for our case. 
The FFT and its sister IFFT is what makes this approach viable: transforming the whole grid takes the same time regardless of how many waves are in it.

Something subtle worth talking about is the conjugate-packing step before the FFT. It ensures the output is in Real values. Without it, you would get complex numbers as the displacement, which is meaningless as geometry. It's an easy detail to look over but it's something I think is interesting, and worth mentioning.

The horizontal displacements (the ih * K / |K| terms) are what separate this from more simple approaches such as heightfield water. Vertices don't just move up and down, they also curl horizontally in the direction the waves are travelling, which produces that characteristic "roll" you see in real ocean swells.

Where those displacements converge hard enough that the surface folds over itself, the "Jacobian" goes negative, and this is how we calculate sea foam.

We overlap 4 textures of varying size (Close up ripples, mid-range chop, large swells, and distant structures), and that forms the mathematical backend for our ocean, and our shader to render it.


















# The Shaders

The vertex shader samples the four displacement texture arrays, and applies the FFT result directly to the mesh's vertex positions. 
For optimizations sake (which seems to be a dying art in AAA games), the mesh itself is a clipmap of concentric LOD rings.
Normals are derived from the slope textures (the spatial derivatives of displacement), then blended with two scrolling detail normal maps for close-up surface texture. A fun detail is that the normal is split into three separate versions for Fresnel, specular, and diffuse (each with its own strength multiplier). Which allows you to tune how agitated each lighting term looks on its own. A calm specular highlight with choppy Fresnel reads very differently than having them locked together.

As stated previously, the foam is driven by the Jacobian computed by the compute shader, and is sampled per fragment, and multiplied against an optional tiling foam texture. It accumulates and decays for added effect.

Reflections are driven by a cubemap, with a sharp sun disc added on top of the reflection direction (that's fun lol). Specular is a full GGX BRDF opposed to Blinn-Phong, which handles the near-glancing sun angles that you want to see in ocean water.

# The Result

I honestly think the results totally speak for themselves. This crazy system, alongside many other ambitious decisions, ultimately led us to give up on releasing anything finished for the Jam. It was still great fun, and I hope to use it in a future project, or maybe place it on the Asset Store.

If you're curious and want to look deeper into this topic, I couldn't recommend Acerola's series on this topic more. It carried me through implementing this system. It will be linked below.

Thank you for reading!

P.S. if you're curious about the pixelated look, this project took advantage of our QRP Extension, there is an article on here regarding that if you're interested.

media.caption: conjugate packing halves the compute time!
a slice of one of four FFT Render Textures.
calm lake example (if your water exists, this system can handle it)

title: Volumetric Fog System
desc:
# Quick Notes

Volumetric fog is one of those things you take for granted, but a performant volumetric fog system (or lack thereof) can make or break a games atmosphere.
Unity's HDRP pipeline has full volumetric fog support, but URP, the multi-platform, and lower end - middle end hardware supporting pipeline, lacks any useful fog.
This had plagued our studio ever since its creation, and implementing a system such as this has never been easy. It was only recently that I finally felt confident enough to write one, and I will briefly detail it here (design choices, basic math behind it, optimizations, etc.)

# Why is fog so hard?

The naive answer to the fog problem is just "render a coloured overlay with some distance consideration and call it a day," that's more or less what Unity's built-in URP fog does. It's cheap, and it's fast. But it looks flat and unconvincing, because real fog isn't a filter applied to a screen. It's the physical process of light scattering through a medium, bouncing off particles, and attenuating as it travels. The colour of fog at any given point is entirely dependent on where the light came from, how far it travelled, and what's actually in the way.

Reproducing all of that properly means sampling density at many points along many rays from the camera out through the scene: a raymarcher. The problem is that running a raymarcher at full res, with enough steps to look smooth, is painfully expensive. Simply: it slaughters your framerate. So every design decision in a system like this is fundamentally about getting the visual quality of an expensive technique at a price you can actually afford.





# On Our Volumetric Fog Implementation

The raymarcher runs at half resolution by default, and then gets composited back over the full-resolution scene. Halving the resolution cuts the fragment cost to a quarter, which is the single biggest performance improvement across the whole system. The catch is that half-res fog produces horrible edge halos where geometry meets fog (a thin sliver of blurred fog bleeds onto nearby surfaces). The fix is a bilateral upsample: when reconstructing the full resolution image, each of the four neighboring fog samples are depth-weighted, so samples from behind a geometry edge are discarded instead of being blended in.

The same depth weighting is applied to the blur passes:
A 9-tap Gaussian blur runs over the fog buffer before compositing, and any tap whose depth differs significantly from the center pixel is thrown out. Which keeps the blur from spreading over/across silhouettes.












For scattering, our shader uses the Henyey-Greenstein phase function, which is the standard approximation used in film and game VFX for how particles scatter light. A single parameter 'g' controls whether the fog is forward-scattering (bright halos around light sources) or back-scattering (brighter when looking away from the light). Sure, it's not physically exact, but it's cheap and controllable.















Temporal jitter using Interleaved Gradient Noise breaks up the banding that low step counts produce. The noise offset advances by the Golden Ratio each frame, cycling through 64 uncorrelated offsets: so no visible pattern ever settles, even at 16 steps.

For density, the system supports global base fog, an exponential height layer, up to 8 local volumes, and a 3-octave FBM noise field for wisps. The local volumes are just world-space boxes, and they operate additively at no extra performance hit (aside from their individual cost). The volume edges fade smoothly via smoothstep, with a per-volume fade fraction so some boxes can be hard, and others can be more akin to clouds.

Lights scatter into the fog automatically. Up to 8 point/spot lights are picked each frame, sorted by luminance-range-distance priority score, so the most visually important ones always win their slots. When the camera moves inside a light's range, the blur radius grows cubically: the closer to the center, the softer the fog buffer (which approximates the look of being inside a visible light volume without any extra passes).

The whole system runs as a URP Renderer Feature, which means it's one checkbox in the renderer asset, and zero changes to any scene materials. We designed it to work optionally alongside our QRPE system, which allows the quantize pass to run before the fog. (Smooth fog, but a quantized world)



# Final Notes

This was a crazy topic to dive down, and this doesn't even do the complexity of a system like this justice. Visual bugs, working with Render Graph, and more.

Next time you play a game with volumetrics, take some time to appreciate the math, and techniques behind getting some light to bounce differently on your screen.

If you'd like to dive deeper, I've included some good starting resources at the bottom. I'd also highly recommend reading the QRP Extension article on here, it's pretty interesting as well.

media.caption: the sun peeking through leaves and volumetric fog (QRPE enabled)
interleaved gradient noise vs other noise.
